en.extract:
The Rio Branco antbird (Cercomacra carbonaria) is a Critically Endangered bird species in subfamily Thamnophilinae of family Thamnophilidae, the "typical antbirds". It is found in Brazil and Guyana.


Taxonomy and systematics
The Rio Branco antbird was described by the English ornithologists Philip Sclater and Osbert Salvin in 1873 and given its current binomial name Cercomacra carbonaria. A 2014 study confirmed what had been earlier suggested, that the Rio Branco antbird and the Mato Grosso (C. melanaria), jet (C. nigricans), and Bananal (C. ferdinandi) antbirds form a clade within genus Cercomacra.
The Rio Branco antbird is monotypic.


Description
The Rio Branco antbird is 14 to 15.5 cm (5.5 to 6.1 in) long; one individual weighed 14.5 g (0.51 oz). Adult males are almost entirely black, with white edges on their outer scapulars, a white patch between them, and a hidden white patch under them. Their wing coverts and tail feathers have white tips and their flight feathers have white inner edges. Females have a brownish gray head and upperparts with a white interscapular patch. Their wings and tail are dark gray with greenish gray edges on the flight feathers. Their wing coverts are blackish gray with white tips and their tail feathers have white tips. Their throat and upper breast are white with dark gray spots, their lower breast and belly are streaked with gray and buff-white, and their sides, flanks, and crissum are buff-gray.


Distribution and habitat
The Rio Branco antbird is found along the Rio Branco from the Ireng River on the Guyana-Brazil border south downstream to the middle Rio Branco in Roraima and also on some of its tributaries. It inhabits the understorey to mid-storey of gallery forest and forested river islands. It almost always occurs in dense, vine-laden thickets. In elevation its range appears to be restricted to between 800 and 900 m (2,600 and 3,000 ft).


Behavior


Movement
The Rio Branco antbird is believed to be a year-round resident throughout its range.


Feeding
The Rio Branco antbird's diet has not been detailed but is known to be insects and probably also includes spiders. Individuals, pairs, and family groups usually forage between about 1 and 7 m (3 and 23 ft) above the ground, though occasionally as high as 10 m (33 ft). They hop among vine tangles and other dense vegetation and usually take prey by gleaning and lunging from a perch and also sometimes with short fluttering flights. They are not known to join mixed-species feeding flocks nor to follow army ants.


Breeding
The Rio Branco antbird's breeding season has not been defined but appears to include from August or before to perhaps October. Its only known nest was a small cup suspended in a branch fork in a leafy shrub 1.5 m (5 ft) above the ground. Both members of a pair defended their territory. Nothing else is known about the species' breeding biology.


Vocalization
The male Rio Branco's song is "a couplet consisting of short buzzy note and longer, clearer note, repeated 4–10 (most often 4–7) times". Females often interject a "loud buzzy note" which the male then answers with a similar, but lower-pitched, note. The species' calls include a "duo or trio of abrupt notes, delivered rapidly, and [an] unclear whine that sounds like a tree branch squeaking in wind".


Status
The IUCN originally in 1988 assessed the Rio Branco antbird as Threatened, then in 1994 as Vulnerable, in 2008 as Near Threatened, and since 2012 as Critically Endangered. It has a very small range and its estimated population of between 6000 and 15,000 mature individuals is believed to be decreasing. "The primary threat is now thought to be accelerating deforestation in the Amazon basin as land is cleared for cattle ranching and soy production, facilitated by expansion of the road network." It is considered locally fairly common but its available habitat is estimated to cover only 723 km2 (280 sq mi). In addition to deforestation for agriculture, fire (both intentional and accidental) has significantly reduced the species' available habitat. It is common in Viruá National Park.


References

The '''Rio Branco antbird''' ('''''Cercomacra carbonaria''''') is a [[Critically Endangered]] [[bird]] [[species]] in subfamily Thamnophilinae of family [[Thamnophilidae]], the "typical antbirds". It is found in [[Brazil]] and [[Guyana]].<ref name=IUCN/><ref name=IOC14.1>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/antbirds/ |title=Antbirds |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 14.1 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=January 2024 |access-date=4 January 2024 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The Rio Branco antbird was [[Species description|described]] by the English ornithologists [[Philip Sclater]] and [[Osbert Salvin]] in 1873 and given its current [[binomial name]] ''Cercomacra carbonaria''.<ref>{{ cite book | last1=Sclater | first1=Philip L. | author-link=Philip Sclater | last2=Salvin | first2=Osbert | author2-link=Osbert Salvin |  year=1873 | title=Nomenclator Avium Neotropicalium | language=Latin | place=London | publisher=Sumptibus Auctorum | pages=73, 161 | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/40220942 }}</ref> A 2014 study confirmed what had been earlier suggested, that the Rio Branco antbird and the [[Mato Grosso antbird|Mato Grosso]] (''C. melanaria''), [[Jet antbird|jet]] (''C. nigricans''), and [[Bananal antbird|Bananal]] (''C. ferdinandi'') antbirds form a [[clade]] within genus ''Cercomacra''.<ref>Tello, J.G., Raposo, M.A., Bates, J.M., Bravo, G.A., Cadena, C.D. and Maldonado-Coelho, M. (2014). Reassessment of the systematics of the widespread Neotropical genus ''Cercomacra'' (Aves: Thamnophilidae). Zool. J. Linn. Soc. 170(3): 546–565.</ref>

The Rio Branco antbird is [[monotypic]].<ref name=IOC14.1/>

==Description==

The Rio Branco antbird is {{convert|14|to|15.5|cm|in|sigfig=2|abbr=on}} long; one individual weighed {{convert|14.5|g|oz|abbr=on}}. Adult males are almost entirely black, with white edges on their outer [[Birdgloss#Scapulars|scapulars]], a white patch between them, and a hidden white patch under them. Their wing [[covert]]s and tail feathers have white tips and their flight feathers have white inner edges. Females have a brownish gray head and upperparts with a white interscapular patch. Their wings and tail are dark gray with greenish gray edges on the flight feathers. Their wing coverts are blackish gray with white tips and their tail feathers have white tips. Their throat and upper breast are white with dark gray spots, their lower breast and belly are streaked with gray and buff-white, and their sides, flanks, and [[crissum]] are buff-gray.<ref name=RBAN-BOW>Zimmer, K., M.L. Isler, and C. J. Sharpe (2020). Rio Branco Antbird (''Cercomacra carbonaria''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.ribant1.01 retrieved July 17, 2024</ref><ref name=vanPerlo>{{cite book | last =van Perlo | first = Ber| title =A Field Guide to the Birds of Brazil | publisher =Oxford University Press | date =2009 | location =New York | pages =252–253 | isbn =978-0-19-530155-7 }}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

The Rio Branco antbird is found along the [[Branco River|Rio Branco]] from the [[Ireng River]] on the Guyana-Brazil border south downstream to the middle Rio Branco in [[Roraima]] and also on some of its tributaries. It inhabits the understorey to mid-storey of [[gallery forest]] and forested river islands. It almost always occurs in dense, vine-laden thickets. In elevation its range appears to be restricted to between {{convert|800|and|900|m|abbr=on}}.<ref name=IUCN/><ref name=RBAN-BOW/><ref name=vanPerlo/>

==Behavior==

===Movement===

The Rio Branco antbird is believed to be a year-round resident throughout its range.<ref name=RBAN-BOW/>

===Feeding===

The Rio Branco antbird's diet has not been detailed but is known to be insects and probably also includes spiders. Individuals, pairs, and family groups usually forage between about {{convert|1|and|7|m|ft|0|abbr=on}} above the ground, though occasionally as high as {{convert|10|m|ft|0|abbr=on}}. They hop among vine tangles and other dense vegetation and usually take prey by gleaning and lunging from a perch and also sometimes with short fluttering flights. They are not known to join [[mixed-species feeding flock]]s nor to follow [[army ant]]s.<ref name=RBAN-BOW/>

===Breeding===

The Rio Branco antbird's breeding season has not been defined but appears to include from August or before to perhaps October. Its only known nest was a small cup suspended in a branch fork in a leafy shrub {{convert|1.5|m|ft|0|abbr=on}} above the ground. Both members of a pair defended their territory. Nothing else is known about the species' breeding biology.<ref name=RBAN-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Cercomacra-carbonaria |species=the Rio Branco antbird}}
===Vocalization===

The male Rio Branco's song is "a couplet consisting of short buzzy note and longer, clearer note, repeated 4–10 (most often 4–7) times". Females often interject a "loud buzzy note" which the male then answers with a similar, but lower-pitched, note. The species' calls include a "duo or trio of abrupt notes, delivered rapidly, and [an] unclear whine that sounds like a tree branch squeaking in wind".<ref name=RBAN-BOW/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] originally in 1988 assessed the Rio Branco antbird as Threatened, then in 1994 as Vulnerable, in 2008 as Near Threatened, and since 2012 as Critically Endangered. It has a very small range and its estimated population of between 6000 and 15,000 mature individuals is believed to be decreasing. "The primary threat is now thought to be accelerating deforestation in the Amazon basin as land is cleared for cattle ranching and soy production, facilitated by expansion of the road network."<ref name=IUCN/> It is considered locally fairly common but its available habitat is estimated to cover only {{convert|723|km2|sqmi|sigfig=2|abbr=on}}. In addition to deforestation for agriculture, fire (both intentional and accidental) has significantly reduced the species' available habitat. It is common in [[Viruá National Park]].<ref name=RBAN-BOW/>
